Appearance
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
54th running of the World Rally Championship
2026 FIA World Rally Championship
Previous
2025
Next
2027
Support series:
FIA WRC2 Championship
FIA WRC3 Championship
FIA Junior WRC Championship
Elfyn Evans is the current drivers' championship leader.
Scott Martin is the current co-drivers' championship leader.
Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT (GR Yaris Rally1 pictured) are the reigning manufacturers' champions.

The 2026 FIA World Rally Championship is the 54th season of the World Rally Championship, an international rallying series organised by the Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile (FIA) and WRC Promoter GmbH. Teams and crews compete for the World Rally Championships for Drivers, Co-drivers and Manufacturers. Crews are free to compete in cars complying with Groups Rally1 to Rally5 regulations; however, only manufacturers competing with Rally1 cars are eligible to score points in the manufacturers' championship. The championship began in January 2026 with the Monte Carlo Rally and is set to conclude in November 2026 with the Rally Saudi Arabia. The series is supported by the WRC2 and WRC3 categories at every round of the championship and by Junior WRC at selected events.

Sébastien Ogier and Vincent Landais are the reigning drivers' and co-drivers' champions, having secured the 2025 championship at the 2025 Rally Saudi Arabia. Toyota are the defending manufacturers' champions.

After the sixth round, Elfyn Evans and Scott Martin respectively lead the drivers' and co-drivers' championship over Takamoto Katsuta and Aaron Johnston by twelve points. Oliver Solberg and Elliott Edmondson are third, a further nineteen points behind. In the manufacturers' championship, the reigning manufacturers' champions Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT hold a 93-point lead over Hyundai Shell Mobis WRT.

Calendar

[edit]
A map showing the locations of the rallies in the 2026 championship. Event headquarters are marked with black dots.

The 2026 season is scheduled to be contested over fourteen rounds across Europe, Africa, South America and Asia.

Round Start date Finish date Rally Rally headquarters Surface Stages Distance Ref.
1 22 January 25 January Rallye Automobile Monte Carlo Gap, Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur, France Mixed[a] 17 339.15 km [1]
2 12 February 15 February Rally Sweden Umeå, Västerbotten County, Sweden Snow 18 300.66 km [2]
3 12 March 15 March Safari Rally Kenya Nairobi, Nakuru County, Kenya Gravel 20 350.52 km [3]
4 9 April 12 April Croatia Rally Rijeka, Primorje-Gorski Kotar County, Croatia Tarmac 20 300.28 km [4]
5 23 April 26 April Rally Islas Canarias Las Palmas, Gran Canaria, Spain Tarmac 18 322.04 km [5]
6 7 May 10 May Rally de Portugal Matosinhos, Porto, Portugal Gravel 23 344.91 km [6]
7 28 May 31 May Rally Japan Toyota, Aichi, Japan Tarmac 20 302.82 km [7]
8 25 June 28 June Acropolis Rally Greece Loutraki, Corinthia, Greece Gravel 17 329.13 km [8]
9 16 July 19 July Rally Estonia Tartu, Estonia Gravel 18 301.80 km [9]
10 30 July 2 August Rally Finland Jyväskylä, Central Finland, Finland Gravel 20 316.60 km [10]
11 27 August 30 August Rally del Paraguay Encarnación, Itapúa, Paraguay Gravel TBA TBA
12 10 September 13 September Rally Chile Concepción, Biobío, Chile Gravel TBA TBA
13 1 October 4 October Rally Italia Sardegna Alghero, Sardinia, Italy Gravel TBA TBA
14 11 November 14 November Rally Saudi Arabia Jeddah, Mecca Province, Saudi Arabia Gravel TBA TBA
Sources:[11][12]

Calendar changes

[edit]
  • The Croatia Rally returned to the championship after missing the 2025 season, replacing the Central European Rally.[13] The rally featured new headquarters in Rijeka, instead of Zagreb and brand new set of mixed tarmac stages.[14]
  • The Italian Rally and Rally Japan swapped calendar slots, and the new Rally di Roma Capitale was confirmed to replace the Rally Italia Sardegna from 2027 onwards.[15]
  • The organisers of the Acropolis Rally have announced the change of its headquarters, with Loutraki to serve as the new base after five years in Lamia.[16]

Entrants

[edit]

The following manufacturers are set to contest the championship under Rally1 regulations.[17] All crews use tyres provided by Hankook.[18]

Rally1 entries eligible to score manufacturer points
Manufacturer Entrant Car No. Driver name Co-driver name Rounds
Ford M-Sport Ford WRT Ford Puma Rally1 55 Josh McErlean Eoin Treacy 1–7
95 Jon Armstrong Shane Byrne 1–7
Hyundai Hyundai Shell Mobis WRT Hyundai i20 N Rally1 4 Esapekka Lappi Enni Mälkönen 2–3
6 Dani Sordo Cándido Carrera 5–6
11 Thierry Neuville Martijn Wydaeghe 1–7
16 Adrien Fourmaux Alexandre Coria 1–7
20 Hayden Paddon John Kennard 1, 4, 7
Toyota Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT Toyota GR Yaris Rally1 1 Sébastien Ogier Vincent Landais 1, 3, 5–7
18 Takamoto Katsuta Aaron Johnston 2, 4
33 Elfyn Evans Scott Martin 1–7
99 Oliver Solberg Elliott Edmondson 1–7
Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT2 Toyota GR Yaris Rally1 5 Sami Pajari Marko Salminen 1–7
Sources:[19][20][21][22][23][24][25]

The following crews entered in Rally1 cars as privateers or under arrangement with the manufacturers.

Rally1 entries ineligible to score manufacturer points
Manufacturer Entrant Car No. Driver name Co-driver name Rounds
Ford M-Sport Ford WRT Ford Puma Rally1 13 Grégoire Munster Louis Louka 1
22 Mārtiņš Sesks Renārs Francis 2, 6
Toyota Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT Toyota GR Yaris Rally1 18 Takamoto Katsuta Aaron Johnston 1, 3, 5–7
37 Lorenzo Bertelli Simone Scattolin 2
Sources:[19][20][21][22][23][24][25]

In detail

[edit]

M-Sport kept the crew of Josh McErlean and Eoin Treacy.[26] Jon Armstrong and Shane Byrne would step up from the European Rally Championship to complete a full Motorsport Ireland Rally Academy lineup for the team, replacing Grégoire Munster and Louis Louka in the second full-time seat.[27] Munster was later confirmed to contest the season opener in a third Rally1 car with the team.[28] Mārtiņš Sesks would continue to run a partial program with the team, contesting seven events during the season.[29]

Esapekka Lappi (left), Dani Sordo (middle) and Hayden Paddon (right) to return to the top tier for part-time campaigns.

Hyundai maintained the services of Thierry Neuville and Martijn Wydaeghe, and of Adrien Fourmaux and Alexandre Coria for entry in all rallies.[30] A third car will be shared by crews led by drivers Esapekka Lappi, Dani Sordo and Hayden Paddon.[31] Paddon is set to make his first appearance in the premium level of the championship since the 2018 Rally Australia.[32] Lappi and Sordo are also due to return after their previous partial campaigns with the team in 2024.[33] Ott Tänak announced his indefinite break from the championship following the end of the 2025 season.[34]

Toyota retained Elfyn Evans and Scott Martin as their full-time competitor, while Sami Pajari and Marko Salminen, as well as Takamoto Katsuta and Aaron Johnston were also retained.[35] Sébastien Ogier and Vincent Landais would continue to run a partial program to contest ten rallies with the team.[36] Kalle Rovanperä announced that he would leave the championship to pursue a career in open-wheel racing, having signed a contract with Toyota Gazoo Racing to compete in the Super Formula Championship.[37] However, he did not rule out a return to rallying in the future.[38] His seat at Toyota was filled by Oliver Solberg, who is under contract to run his first full-time season in the class, having previously run on a part-time basis for Hyundai in 2022.[39]

Regulation changes

[edit]

Following the wide criticism of lengthy itinerary, a minimum of 10 rest hours was introduced into the event.[40] Engines are also allowed to change after the start of a rally, but in doing so, crews would incur a 60-minute time penalty.[41]

Season report

[edit]

Opening rounds

[edit]

The season opener was held under complicated weather conditions, which caught out several crews, including both M-Sport crews of McErlean and Treacy, and of Armstrong and Byrne, meaning M-Sport ended their 24-year point-scoring finish.[42] The rally was won by Solberg and Edmondson in their first rally of their first full-time season in the top-tier.[43] Toyota continued their dominance in the following round, locking a 1–2–3–4 at the end of the rally, with Evans and Martin winning the event.[44]

The tough Safari Rally saw all three Toyota crews nominated to score points retire on Saturday.[45] Despite running outside of top five by the first leg, Katsuta and Johnston took their maiden victory.[46] A similar story unfolded in Croatia, as both championship contenders Solberg and Evans exited early in the rally.[47] Benefiting from avoiding punctures, Neuville and Wydaeghe took the lead,[48] a position they held until the final Power Stage, where they retired after crashing off-road.[49] Katsuta and Johnston subsequently inherited the win and led the championship for the first time in their careers.[50] Paddon returned to the podium for the first time since the 2018 Rally Australia by claiming third.[51]

Mid-season events

[edit]

Toyota dominated the rally of Spain, with Ogier and Landais winning the event.[52] Title contenders Solberg and Edmondson retired on the penultimate stage of the rally when they were in second after crashing into the barrier.[53] They were on course to get a back-to-back victory at the Rally de Portugal until a puncture at the penultimate stage denied their win.[54] Neuville and Wydaeghe subsquently became the new rally leaders, and won the event, the first of the season for Hyundai.[55]

Results and standings

[edit]

Season summary

[edit]
Round Event Winning driver Winning co-driver Winning entrant Winning time Report Ref.
1 Rallye Automobile Monte Carlo Oliver Solberg Elliott Edmondson Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT 4:24:59.0 Report [56][57]
2 Rally Sweden Elfyn Evans Scott Martin Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT 2:35:53.1 Report [58][59]
3 Safari Rally Kenya Takamoto Katsuta Aaron Johnston Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT 3:16:05.6 Report [60][61]
4 Croatia Rally Takamoto Katsuta Aaron Johnston Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT 2:51:15.8 Report [62][63]
5 Rally Islas Canarias Sébastien Ogier Vincent Landais Toyota Gazoo Racing WRT 2:43:18.9 Report [64][65]
6 Rally de Portugal Thierry Neuville Martijn Wydaeghe Hyundai Shell Mobis WRT 3:53:01.7 Report [66][67]
7 Rally Japan Report
8 Acropolis Rally Greece Report
9 Rally Estonia Report
10 Rally Finland Report
11 Rally del Paraguay Report
12 Rally Chile Report
13 Rally Italia Sardegna Report
14 Rally Saudi Arabia Report

Scoring system

[edit]

Points are awarded to the top ten classified finishers in each event.[68] In the manufacturers' championship, teams are eligible to nominate three crews to score points, but these points are only awarded to the top two classified finishers representing a manufacturer and driving a 2025-specification Rally1 car. There are also five bonus points awarded to the winners in an accumulated standings across all Sunday stages, four points for second place, three for third, two for fourth and one for fifth. The same points scale is awarded to the five fastest crews of the Power Stage as well.[69]

Position 1st 2nd 3rd 4th 5th 6th 7th 8th 9th 10th
Overall 25 17 15 12 10 8 6 4 2 1
Sunday 5 4 3 2 1 —N/a
Power Stage 5 4 3 2 1 —N/a

FIA World Rally Championship for Drivers

The driver who records a points-scoring classification would be taken into account for the championship regardless of the categories.

FIA World Rally Championship for Manufacturers

[edit]

Only the best two results of each manufacturer in the respective overall classification by the end of each rally, accumulated position of all Sunday stages and Power Stage at each rally are taken into account for the championship.

Notes

[edit]
  1. ^ The Monte Carlo Rally is run on a tarmac and snow surface.

  40. ^ Howard, Tom (25 November 2025). "Rest periods will be mandatory in the 2026 WRC season". Motorsport.com. Motorsport Network. Retrieved 1 January 2026.
  41. ^ Barry, Luke (25 November 2025). "WRC to introduce mandatory rest hours in 2026". DirtFish. Retrieved 1 January 2026.
  42. ^ Barry, Luke (26 January 2025). "M-Sport's 24-year points-scoring run ends in Monte Carlo". DirtFish. Retrieved 26 January 2025.
  43. ^ "Pelamourgues Powers to WRC3 Glory". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 26 January 2025. Retrieved 26 January 2025.
  44. ^ "Evans takes charge as Rally Sweden battle intensifies". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 15 February 2026. Retrieved 15 February 2026.
  45. ^ "Solberg and Ogier retire on road section, Katsuta inherits Safari lead". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 14 March 2026. Retrieved 12 April 2026.
  46. ^ "Katsuta makes history with maiden WRC victory at Safari Rally Kenya". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 15 March 2026. Retrieved 15 March 2026.
  47. ^ "Pajari leads dramatic Croatia Rally after chaotic Friday morning". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 10 April 2026. Retrieved 12 April 2026.
  48. ^ Lindsay, Alasdair (11 April 2026). "Rock-induced mass punctures give Neuville Croatia lead". DirtFish. Retrieved 12 April 2026.
  49. ^ Lindsay, Alasdair (12 April 2026). "Neuville powerstage crash hands Katsuta Croatia win". DirtFish. Retrieved 12 April 2026.
  50. ^ "Last-stage twist sends Katsuta to Croatia Rally win". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 12 April 2026. Retrieved 12 April 2026.
  51. ^ Chapman, Simon (13 April 2026). "Paddon survives carnage for WRC podium in Croatia". Speedcafe. Retrieved 13 April 2026.
  52. ^ "Ogier wins Rally Islas Canarias as Solberg's challenge ends in heartbreak". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 26 April 2026. Retrieved 27 April 2026.
  53. ^ Barry, Luke (26 April 2026). "Solberg crashes on penultimate stage of Canarias". DirtFish. Retrieved 1 May 2026.
  54. ^ Barry, Luke (10 May 2026). "Ogier: We did the job but didn't get the reward". DirtFish. Retrieved 16 May 2026.
  55. ^ "Neuville wins Portugal after late Toyota heartbreak". wrc.com. WRC Promoter GmbH. 10 May 2026. Retrieved 11 May 2026.
  56. ^ Howard, Tom (25 January 2026). "WRC Monte Carlo: Solberg dominates 'proper Monte' to claim sensational win". autosport.com. Motorsport Network. Retrieved 25 January 2026.
  57. ^ "Final results Rallye Automobile Monte-Carlo 2026". eWRC-results.com. Retrieved 25 January 2026.
  58. ^ Howard, Tom (15 February 2026). "WRC Sweden: Evans storms to victory as Toyota scores 1–2–3–4". autosport.com. Motorsport Network. Retrieved 15 February 2026.
  59. ^ "Final results Rally Sweden 2026". eWRC-results.com. Retrieved 15 February 2026.
  60. ^ Howard, Tom (15 March 2026). "WRC Safari Rally Kenya: Katsuta scores maiden WRC win in brutal Safari". autosport.com. Motorsport Network. Retrieved 15 March 2026.
  61. ^ "Final results Safari Rally Kenya 2026". eWRC-results.com. Retrieved 15 March 2026.
  62. ^ Howard, Tom (12 April 2026). "WRC Croatia: Katsuta wins after final stage crash for Neuville, Lancia takes first WRC2 victory". autosport.com. Motorsport Network. Retrieved 12 April 2026.
  63. ^ "Final results Croatia Rally 2026". eWRC-results.com. Retrieved 12 April 2026.
